- TaskpaneApp with a single ShowTaskpane button (line 64-67)
  - Dev URL https://localhost:3000 (line 30)

  7. Known-bug handling already present

  exportToChangedown.ts:85-97 — try/catch around getTrackedChanges() swallows bug #5535 move-crash, falls through with empty array.
  bridgeClient.ts:382-397 has the same pattern in handleReviewChange.

  ---
  What the plan actually is, stated concretely

  1. Port word-l3-state.ts from changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/ into packages/word-add-in/src/. The file uses @changedown/core which is
  browser-compatible. Rename WordL3State fields as needed. This is largely a move, not a rewrite.
  2. Add observer/enrichment/tick-queue to the add-in — new code, because today the MCP drives all reads and this problem doesn't exist there.
  3. Replace syncEngine.ts's count-polling with the observer.
  4. New bridge methods on add-in side: get_state, list_changes, propose_change (replaces today's apply_change), review_change (exists), reconcile, ping.
   No push/subscribe — MCP clients poll, matching the existing request/response bridge shape.
  5. Rewire word-remote-handlers.ts to proxy every call to the bridge. Delete word-l3-state.ts from MCP server (or leave as thin cache).
  6. Add packages/word-add-in to root workspaces so @changedown/core resolves.
  7. Add vitest to add-in for the new modules.
  8. Manifest adds SharedRuntime 1.1 + lifetime="long" + setStartupBehavior(load).

  One question

  Do you want push/subscribe (add-in sends unsolicited deltas) or polling (MCP clients call get_state periodically)? Today the protocol is strictly
  server→client request/response with no unsolicited messages. Polling matches that. Push requires extending the protocol to accept {type: "event", ...}
  frames with no response needed.

  Given nothing in the current system does push, I'll default to polling unless you say otherwise. Ready to write the plan on that basis — just say go.

✻ Baked for 2m 35s

❯ fine polling but didn't ytou say the WS auto discconects all cleints when a new one connects is that an issue  I still dont understand why the sidebar
   would be a client too shouldn't it just be the server?

⏺ Two concrete answers:

  Q1: Single-client enforcement

  Line 90-93 of word-bridge-server.ts:
  if (this.client) {
    console.error('[WordBridge] replacing existing add-in connection');
    this.client.close();
  }
  this.client = socket;

  Not a problem for the add-in. The add-in reconnects after disconnect (bridgeClient.ts:147-153 auto-reconnect every 3s). When it reconnects, the old
  dead socket is kicked. Only one live add-in, always. Good.

  Only a problem if you want multiple MCP clients connected to the bridge simultaneously — which leads to your second question.

  Q2: Why isn't the sidebar the server

  Hard browser rule: task panes can't bind listening sockets. WebView2 / WKWebView / Word Online iframe — none can be WebSocket servers. new
  WebSocket(url) is client-only. new WebSocketServer() needs node:net, which browsers don't expose.

  So the sidebar is a client of the transport, but owner of the state — the protocol authority. The bridge is stateless plumbing.

  Where the bridge runs is the real architectural choice:

  Option A — current setup (bridge inside MCP server process). The MCP server spawns WordBridgeServer on port 3001 (mcp-server/src/index.ts:85). Exactly
  one MCP server process, one add-in. "Any MCP can connect" = "whichever MCP is running at the time." Serial, not simultaneous.

  Option B — standalone bridge daemon. Bridge becomes its own changedown-bridge process, started on demand. The add-in connects to it. Every MCP server
  process that wants Word access also connects to it as a client. Bridge has a routing table — messages from any MCP client get forwarded to the add-in;
  responses get sent back to the originating MCP client. Single-client enforcement at line 90-93 gets replaced with a client registry (add-in slot +
  MCP-client map). This supports true simultaneous multi-MCP.

  Which do you want? A is what exists and requires zero transport changes; B is more work but lets multiple Claude/agent processes share the same Word
  session live.
           Claude Code v2.1.111
 ▐▛███▜▌   Opus 4.7 (1M context) with xhigh effort · Claude Max
▝▜█████▛▘  ~/Coding/changetracks
  ▘▘ ▝▝    Welcome to Opus 4.7 xhigh! · /effort to tune speed vs. intelligence

⏺ Before I write the plan, I hit a spec-level issue that needs to be resolved first. It changes the architecture.

  The issue: sidebars can't be WebSocket servers

  The spec says "WebSocket server bound to wss://localhost:<port> inside the sidebar." But browsers (including WebView2/WKWebView/Word Online
  iframe) cannot open listening sockets. A task pane is unavoidably a WebSocket client, not a server. This was in the research earlier — I missed
  its implications when writing the spec.

  The existing bridgeClient.ts already reflects this: it's a client connecting to wss://localhost:3001, not a server.

  What this means: for MCP clients to reach the sidebar's L3 state, there has to be a broker / bridge process that the sidebar connects to as a
  client, and that MCP clients also connect to as clients. The broker is stateless — just a pass-through — but it's a separate deliverable.

  Three realistic options to resolve:

  A. Plan includes building a minimal local broker (Node.js, ws library, ~200 lines). Installed as part of the ChangeDown toolchain. Sidebar + MCP
   clients both connect to it as clients. Broker routes messages. Scope goes up but stays coherent.

  B. Reuse/extend whatever backs the existing bridgeClient.ts → wss://localhost:3001. I'd need to understand what's running that port today —
  presumably an existing MCP server. Could be elegant if that server already exists in this repo.

  C. Rethink: have MCP clients spawn the sidebar or hand it a reverse-tunneling mechanism. Not realistic — adds way more complexity.

  I recommend B if there's already a local WebSocket server in the repo we can extend, otherwise A.

  How do you want to proceed? Or should I go look at what's on wss://localhost:3001 in the current implementation first and then come back with a
  concrete recommendation?

✻ Cooked for 4m 57s

❯ what atre you talking about the current version lets me connect to it, what is that

  Searched for 3 patterns, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ You were right. I missed it. Current architecture:

  - changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-bridge-server.ts — Node-side WebSocket SERVER on wss://localhost:3001 using ws lib, with TLS from Office
   dev certs.
  - The Word add-in (bridgeClient.ts) connects to it as a client.
  - Today the bridge is used in one direction: MCP server pushes commands to the add-in (apply_change, review_change, read_document) and the
  add-in executes Word JS API calls and responds.
  - Only one add-in connection at a time; the server is part of the MCP server process.

  So the broker already exists. I was spinning up architectural anxiety over something that's already built.

  Plan adjustment: We extend word-bridge-server.ts to also broker sidebar → MCP client traffic (the new direction):
  - Sidebar holds L3 state, pushes deltas to the bridge
  - Additional MCP clients connect to the bridge as peers
  - Bridge relays get_state / subscribe / propose_change / review_change / ping between them
  - Remains stateless — state still lives in the sidebar

  The "wss://localhost: inside the sidebar" phrasing in the spec should read "sidebar connects to the local ChangeDown bridge at
  wss://localhost:3001 and serves L3 state through it; MCP clients connect to the same bridge." Minor spec wording fix; architecture unchanged
  from what you already have, just extended.

  Want me to:

  A. Fix the spec wording (one-line surgical edit) and then write the plan
  B. Skip the spec edit and just write the plan with the correct architecture baked in
  C. Something else

  Read 17 files, listed 1 directory (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Proof. Every claim cites a file:line or exact code I read.

  1. The bridge

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-bridge-server.ts — a WebSocketServer at wss://localhost:3001 (line 70-72, TLS via
  ~/.office-addin-dev-certs; line 75 fallback to plain ws://). Single-client — line 90-93 closes any existing connection when a new one arrives.
  Method .call(method, params, timeoutMs) at line 125 sends BridgeRequest {id, method, params}, waits for matching BridgeResponse {id, ok,
  result?, error?} by UUID.

  packages/word-add-in/src/taskpane/bridgeClient.ts — add-in connects to wss://localhost:3001 at line 73, auto-reconnects every 3s (line 74).
  handleMessage/dispatch at lines 157-217 currently dispatch three methods: read_document, apply_change, review_change (lines 194-205).

  The protocol is already bidirectional in shape — server pushes requests, client responds. The direction "who owns state" is not encoded in the
  transport.

  2. Where L3 state lives today — in the MCP server, not the add-in

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-l3-state.ts owns the complete L3 state:

  - WordL3State at lines 45-56: lines[], hashes[], footnoteLog: Map<cnId, WordL3FootnoteEntry>, cnCounter.
  - initFromReadDocument(lines) (line 84): refreshes body + hashes on each read_document response.
  - registerChange(cnId, type, author, date, paraIndex, oldText, newText, column?) (line 99): builds contextual edit-op via
  buildContextualL3EditOp (imported from @changedown/core, line 16) and inserts into footnoteLog.
  - ensureFreshAnchor(cnId) (line 178): fast path hash match → hash relocation → runScrubReplay.
  - runScrubReplay() (line 212): uses scrubBackward/scrubForward from @changedown/core (lines 17-18), writes fresh anchors back to entries.
  - updateStatus(cnId, 'accepted'|'rejected') (line 270).
  - getProposedChanges() (line 290) reads directly from footnoteLog.

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-remote-handlers.ts — the MCP tool handlers drive everything:

  - wordReadTrackedFile (line 103) → bridge.call('read_document') → initFromReadDocument(lines) → formats hashline output.
  - wordProposeChange (line 137) → validates at via parseAt + computeLineHash, relocates on mismatch (line 181-204), → bridge.call('apply_change',
   {cnId, type, oldText, newText, paragraphIndex, author, date}) → registerChange.
  - wordListChanges (line 273) → no bridge call — reads directly from getProposedChanges().
  - wordReviewChanges (line 304) → ensureFreshAnchor(change_id) → bridge.call('review_change', {cnId, decision, paragraphIndex, searchTexts}) →
  updateStatus.

  3. Add-in today has no L3 state

  - packages/word-add-in/src/taskpane/syncEngine.ts (lines 93-126): 3-second poll of body.getTrackedChanges().items.length — tracks count only, no
   semantic processing.
  - stateManager.ts: only persists comment IDs, author map, last-sync timestamp in document.settings.
  - No observer, no diff engine, no tick queue, no scrub replay. Add-in is effectively stateless.

  4. @changedown/core

  Package at packages/core — builds to dist/ (CJS) + dist-esm/ (ESM), both declared in exports (package.json lines 29-34). Exports used by
  word-l3-state.ts: initHashline, computeLineHash, buildContextualL3EditOp, scrubBackward, scrubForward, ActiveOperation, ContextualEditOpParams.
  Also exports parseAt, parseOp used by word-remote-handlers.ts (line 25-27).

  5. Workspace gap

  Root package.json lines 6-24 — packages/word-add-in is NOT in workspaces. Add-in's package.json has no @changedown/core dep. Today the add-in
  never imports core.

  6. Manifest today

  packages/word-add-in/manifest.xml:
  - WordApi MinVersion 1.6 (line 23)
  - No <Runtimes> element, no lifetime="long", no LaunchEvent
  - TaskpaneApp with a single ShowTaskpane button (line 64-67)
  - Dev URL https://localhost:3000 (line 30)

  7. Known-bug handling already present

  exportToChangedown.ts:85-97 — try/catch around getTrackedChanges() swallows bug #5535 move-crash, falls through with empty array.
  bridgeClient.ts:382-397 has the same pattern in handleReviewChange.

  browser-compatible. Rename WordL3State fields as needed. This is largely a move, not a rewrite.

  reconcile, ping. No push/subscribe — MCP clients poll, matching the existing request/response bridge shape.
  5. Rewire word-remote-handlers.ts to proxy every call to the bridge. Delete word-l3-state.ts from MCP server (or leave as thin cache).
  6. Add packages/word-add-in to root workspaces so @changedown/core resolves.
  7. Add vitest to add-in for the new modules.
  8. Manifest adds SharedRuntime 1.1 + lifetime="long" + setStartupBehavior(load).

  One question

  Do you want push/subscribe (add-in sends unsolicited deltas) or polling (MCP clients call get_state periodically)? Today the protocol is
   strictly server→client request/response with no unsolicited messages. Polling matches that. Push requires extending the protocol to
  accept {type: "event", ...} frames with no response needed.

  Given nothing in the current system does push, I'll default to polling unless you say otherwise. Ready to write the plan on that basis —
   just say go.

✻ Baked for 2m 35s

❯ fine polling but didn't ytou say the WS auto discconects all cleints when a new one connects is that an issue  I still dont understand
  why the sidebar would be a client too shouldn't it just be the server?

⏺ Two concrete answers:

  Q1: Single-client enforcement

  Line 90-93 of word-bridge-server.ts:
  if (this.client) {
    console.error('[WordBridge] replacing existing add-in connection');
    this.client.close();
  }
  this.client = socket;

  Not a problem for the add-in. The add-in reconnects after disconnect (bridgeClient.ts:147-153 auto-reconnect every 3s). When it
  reconnects, the old dead socket is kicked. Only one live add-in, always. Good.

  Only a problem if you want multiple MCP clients connected to the bridge simultaneously — which leads to your second question.

  Q2: Why isn't the sidebar the server

  Hard browser rule: task panes can't bind listening sockets. WebView2 / WKWebView / Word Online iframe — none can be WebSocket servers.
  new WebSocket(url) is client-only. new WebSocketServer() needs node:net, which browsers don't expose.

  So the sidebar is a client of the transport, but owner of the state — the protocol authority. The bridge is stateless plumbing.

  Where the bridge runs is the real architectural choice:

  Option A — current setup (bridge inside MCP server process). The MCP server spawns WordBridgeServer on port 3001
  (mcp-server/src/index.ts:85). Exactly one MCP server process, one add-in. "Any MCP can connect" = "whichever MCP is running at the
  time." Serial, not simultaneous.

  Option B — standalone bridge daemon. Bridge becomes its own changedown-bridge process, started on demand. The add-in connects to it.
  Every MCP server process that wants Word access also connects to it as a client. Bridge has a routing table — messages from any MCP
  client get forwarded to the add-in; responses get sent back to the originating MCP client. Single-client enforcement at line 90-93 gets
  replaced with a client registry (add-in slot + MCP-client map). This supports true simultaneous multi-MCP.

  Which do you want? A is what exists and requires zero transport changes; B is more work but lets multiple Claude/agent processes share
  the same Word session live.
           Claude Code v2.1.111
 ▐▛███▜▌   Opus 4.7 (1M context) with xhigh effort · Claude Max
▝▜█████▛▘  ~/Coding/changetracks
  ▘▘ ▝▝    Welcome to Opus 4.7 xhigh! · /effort to tune speed vs. intelligence

⏺ Before I write the plan, I hit a spec-level issue that needs to be resolved first. It changes the architecture.

  The issue: sidebars can't be WebSocket servers

  The spec says "WebSocket server bound to wss://localhost:<port> inside the sidebar." But browsers (including
  WebView2/WKWebView/Word Online iframe) cannot open listening sockets. A task pane is unavoidably a WebSocket client, not a server.
  This was in the research earlier — I missed its implications when writing the spec.

  The existing bridgeClient.ts already reflects this: it's a client connecting to wss://localhost:3001, not a server.

  What this means: for MCP clients to reach the sidebar's L3 state, there has to be a broker / bridge process that the sidebar
  connects to as a client, and that MCP clients also connect to as clients. The broker is stateless — just a pass-through — but it's
  a separate deliverable.

  Three realistic options to resolve:

  A. Plan includes building a minimal local broker (Node.js, ws library, ~200 lines). Installed as part of the ChangeDown toolchain.
  Sidebar + MCP clients both connect to it as clients. Broker routes messages. Scope goes up but stays coherent.

  B. Reuse/extend whatever backs the existing bridgeClient.ts → wss://localhost:3001. I'd need to understand what's running that port
   today — presumably an existing MCP server. Could be elegant if that server already exists in this repo.

  C. Rethink: have MCP clients spawn the sidebar or hand it a reverse-tunneling mechanism. Not realistic — adds way more complexity.

  I recommend B if there's already a local WebSocket server in the repo we can extend, otherwise A.

  How do you want to proceed? Or should I go look at what's on wss://localhost:3001 in the current implementation first and then come
   back with a concrete recommendation?

❯ what atre you talking about the current version lets me connect to it, what is that

  Searched for 3 patterns, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ You were right. I missed it. Current architecture:

  - changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-bridge-server.ts — Node-side WebSocket SERVER on wss://localhost:3001 using ws lib, with
  TLS from Office dev certs.
  - The Word add-in (bridgeClient.ts) connects to it as a client.
  - Today the bridge is used in one direction: MCP server pushes commands to the add-in (apply_change, review_change, read_document)
  and the add-in executes Word JS API calls and responds.
  - Only one add-in connection at a time; the server is part of the MCP server process.

  So the broker already exists. I was spinning up architectural anxiety over something that's already built.

  Plan adjustment: We extend word-bridge-server.ts to also broker sidebar → MCP client traffic (the new direction):
  - Sidebar holds L3 state, pushes deltas to the bridge
  - Additional MCP clients connect to the bridge as peers
  - Bridge relays get_state / subscribe / propose_change / review_change / ping between them
  - Remains stateless — state still lives in the sidebar

  The "wss://localhost: inside the sidebar" phrasing in the spec should read "sidebar connects to the local ChangeDown bridge at
  wss://localhost:3001 and serves L3 state through it; MCP clients connect to the same bridge." Minor spec wording fix; architecture
  unchanged from what you already have, just extended.

  Want me to:

  A. Fix the spec wording (one-line surgical edit) and then write the plan
  B. Skip the spec edit and just write the plan with the correct architecture baked in
  C. Something else

⏺ You're right. I'm going to read every single piece I plan to touch — no more theorizing. Parallel reads:

  Read 17 files, listed 1 directory (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Proof. Every claim cites a file:line or exact code I read.

  1. The bridge

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-bridge-server.ts — a WebSocketServer at wss://localhost:3001 (line 70-72, TLS via
  ~/.office-addin-dev-certs; line 75 fallback to plain ws://). Single-client — line 90-93 closes any existing connection when a new
  one arrives. Method .call(method, params, timeoutMs) at line 125 sends BridgeRequest {id, method, params}, waits for matching
  BridgeResponse {id, ok, result?, error?} by UUID.

  packages/word-add-in/src/taskpane/bridgeClient.ts — add-in connects to wss://localhost:3001 at line 73, auto-reconnects every 3s
  (line 74). handleMessage/dispatch at lines 157-217 currently dispatch three methods: read_document, apply_change, review_change
  (lines 194-205).

  The protocol is already bidirectional in shape — server pushes requests, client responds. The direction "who owns state" is not
  encoded in the transport.

  2. Where L3 state lives today — in the MCP server, not the add-in

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-l3-state.ts owns the complete L3 state:

  - WordL3State at lines 45-56: lines[], hashes[], footnoteLog: Map<cnId, WordL3FootnoteEntry>, cnCounter.
  - initFromReadDocument(lines) (line 84): refreshes body + hashes on each read_document response.
  - registerChange(cnId, type, author, date, paraIndex, oldText, newText, column?) (line 99): builds contextual edit-op via
  buildContextualL3EditOp (imported from @changedown/core, line 16) and inserts into footnoteLog.
  - ensureFreshAnchor(cnId) (line 178): fast path hash match → hash relocation → runScrubReplay.
  - runScrubReplay() (line 212): uses scrubBackward/scrubForward from @changedown/core (lines 17-18), writes fresh anchors back to
  entries.
  - updateStatus(cnId, 'accepted'|'rejected') (line 270).
  - getProposedChanges() (line 290) reads directly from footnoteLog.

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-remote-handlers.ts — the MCP tool handlers drive everything:

  - wordReadTrackedFile (line 103) → bridge.call('read_document') → initFromReadDocument(lines) → formats hashline output.
  - wordProposeChange (line 137) → validates at via parseAt + computeLineHash, relocates on mismatch (line 181-204), →
  bridge.call('apply_change', {cnId, type, oldText, newText, paragraphIndex, author, date}) → registerChange.
  - wordListChanges (line 273) → no bridge call — reads directly from getProposedChanges().
  - wordReviewChanges (line 304) → ensureFreshAnchor(change_id) → bridge.call('review_change', {cnId, decision, paragraphIndex,
  searchTexts}) → updateStatus.

  3. Add-in today has no L3 state

  - packages/word-add-in/src/taskpane/syncEngine.ts (lines 93-126): 3-second poll of body.getTrackedChanges().items.length — tracks
  count only, no semantic processing.
  - stateManager.ts: only persists comment IDs, author map, last-sync timestamp in document.settings.
  - No observer, no diff engine, no tick queue, no scrub replay. Add-in is effectively stateless.

  4. @changedown/core

  Package at packages/core — builds to dist/ (CJS) + dist-esm/ (ESM), both declared in exports (package.json lines 29-34). Exports
  used by word-l3-state.ts: initHashline, computeLineHash, buildContextualL3EditOp, scrubBackward, scrubForward, ActiveOperation,
  ContextualEditOpParams. Also exports parseAt, parseOp used by word-remote-handlers.ts (line 25-27).

  5. Workspace gap

  Root package.json lines 6-24 — packages/word-add-in is NOT in workspaces. Add-in's package.json has no @changedown/core dep. Today
  the add-in never imports core.

  6. Manifest today

  packages/word-add-in/manifest.xml:
  - WordApi MinVersion 1.6 (line 23)
  - No <Runtimes> element, no lifetime="long", no LaunchEvent
  - TaskpaneApp with a single ShowTaskpane button (line 64-67)
  - Dev URL https://localhost:3000 (line 30)

  7. Known-bug handling already present

  exportToChangedown.ts:85-97 — try/catch around getTrackedChanges() swallows bug #5535 move-crash, falls through with empty array.
  bridgeClient.ts:382-397 has the same pattern in handleReviewChange.

  ---
  What the plan actually is, stated concretely

  1. Port word-l3-state.ts from changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/ into packages/word-add-in/src/. The file uses @changedown/core
  which is browser-compatible. Rename WordL3State fields as needed. This is largely a move, not a rewrite.
  2. Add observer/enrichment/tick-queue to the add-in — new code, because today the MCP drives all reads and this problem doesn't
  exist there.
  3. Replace syncEngine.ts's count-polling with the observer.
  4. New bridge methods on add-in side: get_state, list_changes, propose_change (replaces today's apply_change), review_change
  (exists), reconcile, ping. No push/subscribe — MCP clients poll, matching the existing request/response bridge shape.
  5. Rewire word-remote-handlers.ts to proxy every call to the bridge. Delete word-l3-state.ts from MCP server (or leave as thin
  cache).
  6. Add packages/word-add-in to root workspaces so @changedown/core resolves.
  7. Add vitest to add-in for the new modules.
  8. Manifest adds SharedRuntime 1.1 + lifetime="long" + setStartupBehavior(load).

  One question

  Do you want push/subscribe (add-in sends unsolicited deltas) or polling (MCP clients call get_state periodically)? Today the
  protocol is strictly server→client request/response with no unsolicited messages. Polling matches that. Push requires extending the
   protocol to accept {type: "event", ...} frames with no response needed.

  Given nothing in the current system does push, I'll default to polling unless you say otherwise. Ready to write the plan on that
  basis — just say go.

  understand why the sidebar would be a client too shouldn't it just be the server?

⏺ Two concrete answers:

  Q1: Single-client enforcement

  Line 90-93 of word-bridge-server.ts:
  if (this.client) {
    console.error('[WordBridge] replacing existing add-in connection');
    this.client.close();
  }
  this.client = socket;

  Not a problem for the add-in. The add-in reconnects after disconnect (bridgeClient.ts:147-153 auto-reconnect every 3s). When it
  reconnects, the old dead socket is kicked. Only one live add-in, always. Good.

  Only a problem if you want multiple MCP clients connected to the bridge simultaneously — which leads to your second question.

  Q2: Why isn't the sidebar the server

  Hard browser rule: task panes can't bind listening sockets. WebView2 / WKWebView / Word Online iframe — none can be WebSocket
  servers. new WebSocket(url) is client-only. new WebSocketServer() needs node:net, which browsers don't expose.

  So the sidebar is a client of the transport, but owner of the state — the protocol authority. The bridge is stateless plumbing.

  Where the bridge runs is the real architectural choice:

  Option A — current setup (bridge inside MCP server process). The MCP server spawns WordBridgeServer on port 3001
  (mcp-server/src/index.ts:85). Exactly one MCP server process, one add-in. "Any MCP can connect" = "whichever MCP is running at
  the time." Serial, not simultaneous.

  Option B — standalone bridge daemon. Bridge becomes its own changedown-bridge process, started on demand. The add-in connects to
  it. Every MCP server process that wants Word access also connects to it as a client. Bridge has a routing table — messages from
  any MCP client get forwarded to the add-in; responses get sent back to the originating MCP client. Single-client enforcement at
  line 90-93 gets replaced with a client registry (add-in slot + MCP-client map). This supports true simultaneous multi-MCP.

  Which do you want? A is what exists and requires zero transport changes; B is more work but lets multiple Claude/agent processes
  share the same Word session live.
           Claude Code v2.1.111
 ▐▛███▜▌   Opus 4.7 (1M context) with xhigh effort · Claude Max
▝▜█████▛▘  ~/Coding/changetracks
  ▘▘ ▝▝    Welcome to Opus 4.7 xhigh! · /effort to tune speed vs. intelligence

⏺ Before I write the plan, I hit a spec-level issue that needs to be resolved first. It changes the architecture.

  The issue: sidebars can't be WebSocket servers

  The spec says "WebSocket server bound to wss://localhost:<port> inside the sidebar." But browsers (including
  WebView2/WKWebView/Word Online iframe) cannot open listening sockets. A task pane is unavoidably a WebSocket client, not a
  server. This was in the research earlier — I missed its implications when writing the spec.

  The existing bridgeClient.ts already reflects this: it's a client connecting to wss://localhost:3001, not a server.

  What this means: for MCP clients to reach the sidebar's L3 state, there has to be a broker / bridge process that the sidebar
  connects to as a client, and that MCP clients also connect to as clients. The broker is stateless — just a pass-through — but
  it's a separate deliverable.

  Three realistic options to resolve:

  A. Plan includes building a minimal local broker (Node.js, ws library, ~200 lines). Installed as part of the ChangeDown
  toolchain. Sidebar + MCP clients both connect to it as clients. Broker routes messages. Scope goes up but stays coherent.

  B. Reuse/extend whatever backs the existing bridgeClient.ts → wss://localhost:3001. I'd need to understand what's running that
  port today — presumably an existing MCP server. Could be elegant if that server already exists in this repo.

  C. Rethink: have MCP clients spawn the sidebar or hand it a reverse-tunneling mechanism. Not realistic — adds way more
  complexity.

  I recommend B if there's already a local WebSocket server in the repo we can extend, otherwise A.

  How do you want to proceed? Or should I go look at what's on wss://localhost:3001 in the current implementation first and then
  come back with a concrete recommendation?

✻ Cooked for 4m 57s

❯ what atre you talking about the current version lets me connect to it, what is that

  Searched for 3 patterns, read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ You were right. I missed it. Current architecture:

  - changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-bridge-server.ts — Node-side WebSocket SERVER on wss://localhost:3001 using ws lib, with
   TLS from Office dev certs.
  - The Word add-in (bridgeClient.ts) connects to it as a client.
  - Today the bridge is used in one direction: MCP server pushes commands to the add-in (apply_change, review_change,
  read_document) and the add-in executes Word JS API calls and responds.
  - Only one add-in connection at a time; the server is part of the MCP server process.

  So the broker already exists. I was spinning up architectural anxiety over something that's already built.

  Plan adjustment: We extend word-bridge-server.ts to also broker sidebar → MCP client traffic (the new direction):
  - Sidebar holds L3 state, pushes deltas to the bridge
  - Additional MCP clients connect to the bridge as peers
  - Bridge relays get_state / subscribe / propose_change / review_change / ping between them
  - Remains stateless — state still lives in the sidebar

  The "wss://localhost: inside the sidebar" phrasing in the spec should read "sidebar connects to the local ChangeDown bridge at
  wss://localhost:3001 and serves L3 state through it; MCP clients connect to the same bridge." Minor spec wording fix;
  architecture unchanged from what you already have, just extended.

  Want me to:

  A. Fix the spec wording (one-line surgical edit) and then write the plan
  B. Skip the spec edit and just write the plan with the correct architecture baked in
  C. Something else

⏺ You're right. I'm going to read every single piece I plan to touch — no more theorizing. Parallel reads:

  Read 17 files, listed 1 directory (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Proof. Every claim cites a file:line or exact code I read.

  1. The bridge

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-bridge-server.ts — a WebSocketServer at wss://localhost:3001 (line 70-72, TLS via
  ~/.office-addin-dev-certs; line 75 fallback to plain ws://). Single-client — line 90-93 closes any existing connection when a
  new one arrives. Method .call(method, params, timeoutMs) at line 125 sends BridgeRequest {id, method, params}, waits for
  matching BridgeResponse {id, ok, result?, error?} by UUID.

  packages/word-add-in/src/taskpane/bridgeClient.ts — add-in connects to wss://localhost:3001 at line 73, auto-reconnects every 3s
   (line 74). handleMessage/dispatch at lines 157-217 currently dispatch three methods: read_document, apply_change, review_change
   (lines 194-205).

  The protocol is already bidirectional in shape — server pushes requests, client responds. The direction "who owns state" is not
  encoded in the transport.

  2. Where L3 state lives today — in the MCP server, not the add-in

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-l3-state.ts owns the complete L3 state:

  - WordL3State at lines 45-56: lines[], hashes[], footnoteLog: Map<cnId, WordL3FootnoteEntry>, cnCounter.
  - initFromReadDocument(lines) (line 84): refreshes body + hashes on each read_document response.
  - registerChange(cnId, type, author, date, paraIndex, oldText, newText, column?) (line 99): builds contextual edit-op via
  buildContextualL3EditOp (imported from @changedown/core, line 16) and inserts into footnoteLog.
  - ensureFreshAnchor(cnId) (line 178): fast path hash match → hash relocation → runScrubReplay.
  - runScrubReplay() (line 212): uses scrubBackward/scrubForward from @changedown/core (lines 17-18), writes fresh anchors back to
   entries.
  - updateStatus(cnId, 'accepted'|'rejected') (line 270).
  - getProposedChanges() (line 290) reads directly from footnoteLog.

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-remote-handlers.ts — the MCP tool handlers drive everything:

  - wordReadTrackedFile (line 103) → bridge.call('read_document') → initFromReadDocument(lines) → formats hashline output.
  - wordProposeChange (line 137) → validates at via parseAt + computeLineHash, relocates on mismatch (line 181-204), →
  bridge.call('apply_change', {cnId, type, oldText, newText, paragraphIndex, author, date}) → registerChange.
  - wordListChanges (line 273) → no bridge call — reads directly from getProposedChanges().
  - wordReviewChanges (line 304) → ensureFreshAnchor(change_id) → bridge.call('review_change', {cnId, decision, paragraphIndex,
  searchTexts}) → updateStatus.

  3. Add-in today has no L3 state

  - packages/word-add-in/src/taskpane/syncEngine.ts (lines 93-126): 3-second poll of body.getTrackedChanges().items.length —
  tracks count only, no semantic processing.
  - stateManager.ts: only persists comment IDs, author map, last-sync timestamp in document.settings.
  - No observer, no diff engine, no tick queue, no scrub replay. Add-in is effectively stateless.

  4. @changedown/core

  Package at packages/core — builds to dist/ (CJS) + dist-esm/ (ESM), both declared in exports (package.json lines 29-34). Exports
   used by word-l3-state.ts: initHashline, computeLineHash, buildContextualL3EditOp, scrubBackward, scrubForward, ActiveOperation,
   ContextualEditOpParams. Also exports parseAt, parseOp used by word-remote-handlers.ts (line 25-27).

  5. Workspace gap

  Root package.json lines 6-24 — packages/word-add-in is NOT in workspaces. Add-in's package.json has no @changedown/core dep.
  Today the add-in never imports core.

  6. Manifest today

  packages/word-add-in/manifest.xml:
  - WordApi MinVersion 1.6 (line 23)
  - No <Runtimes> element, no lifetime="long", no LaunchEvent
  - TaskpaneApp with a single ShowTaskpane button (line 64-67)
  - Dev URL https://localhost:3000 (line 30)

  7. Known-bug handling already present

  exportToChangedown.ts:85-97 — try/catch around getTrackedChanges() swallows bug #5535 move-crash, falls through with empty
  array. bridgeClient.ts:382-397 has the same pattern in handleReviewChange.

  ---
  What the plan actually is, stated concretely

  1. Port word-l3-state.ts from changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/ into packages/word-add-in/src/. The file uses @changedown/core
  which is browser-compatible. Rename WordL3State fields as needed. This is largely a move, not a rewrite.
  2. Add observer/enrichment/tick-queue to the add-in — new code, because today the MCP drives all reads and this problem doesn't
  exist there.
  3. Replace syncEngine.ts's count-polling with the observer.
  4. New bridge methods on add-in side: get_state, list_changes, propose_change (replaces today's apply_change), review_change
  (exists), reconcile, ping. No push/subscribe — MCP clients poll, matching the existing request/response bridge shape.
  5. Rewire word-remote-handlers.ts to proxy every call to the bridge. Delete word-l3-state.ts from MCP server (or leave as thin
  cache).
  6. Add packages/word-add-in to root workspaces so @changedown/core resolves.
  7. Add vitest to add-in for the new modules.
  8. Manifest adds SharedRuntime 1.1 + lifetime="long" + setStartupBehavior(load).

  One question

  Do you want push/subscribe (add-in sends unsolicited deltas) or polling (MCP clients call get_state periodically)? Today the
  protocol is strictly server→client request/response with no unsolicited messages. Polling matches that. Push requires extending
  the protocol to accept {type: "event", ...} frames with no response needed.

  Given nothing in the current system does push, I'll default to polling unless you say otherwise. Ready to write the plan on that
   basis — just say go.

  ⎿  Wrote 4411 lines to docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-16-word-sidebar-l3-state.md
      3 > **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or
        ement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.
      5 **Goal:** Invert Word add-in's change-tracking architecture so the sidebar owns a live in-memory L3 document, served
        t MCP clients through a standalone bridge daemon.
      7 **Architecture:** The Word task pane becomes the authoritative L3 state owner. A new standalone `changedown-bridge`
        host:3001` and routes between exactly one add-in (the state holder) and many MCP-server clients (the state queriers).

        ay, and enriches with Word's `TrackedChange` metadata (author/date) best-effort. MCP clients poll — no
      9 **Tech Stack:** TypeScript, Office.js (WordApi 1.6+, SharedRuntime 1.1), `@changedown/core` (hashline, contextual
         (Node WebSocket lib for daemon + M

⏺ You're right. I'm going to read every single piece I plan to touch — no more theorizing. Parallel reads:

  Read 17 files, listed 1 directory (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ Proof. Every claim cites a file:line or exact code I read.

  1. The bridge

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-bridge-server.ts — a WebSocketServer at wss://localhost:3001 (line 70-72, TLS via
  ~/.office-addin-dev-certs; line 75 fallback to plain ws://). Single-client — line 90-93 closes any existing connection when a
  new one arrives. Method .call(method, params, timeoutMs) at line 125 sends BridgeRequest {id, method, params}, waits for
  matching BridgeResponse {id, ok, result?, error?} by UUID.

  packages/word-add-in/src/taskpane/bridgeClient.ts — add-in connects to wss://localhost:3001 at line 73, auto-reconnects every 3s
   (line 74). handleMessage/dispatch at lines 157-217 currently dispatch three methods: read_document, apply_change, review_change
   (lines 194-205).

  The protocol is already bidirectional in shape — server pushes requests, client responds. The direction "who owns state" is not
  encoded in the transport.

  2. Where L3 state lives today — in the MCP server, not the add-in

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-l3-state.ts owns the complete L3 state:

  - WordL3State at lines 45-56: lines[], hashes[], footnoteLog: Map<cnId, WordL3FootnoteEntry>, cnCounter.
  - initFromReadDocument(lines) (line 84): refreshes body + hashes on each read_document response.
  - registerChange(cnId, type, author, date, paraIndex, oldText, newText, column?) (line 99): builds contextual edit-op via
  buildContextualL3EditOp (imported from @changedown/core, line 16) and inserts into footnoteLog.
  - ensureFreshAnchor(cnId) (line 178): fast path hash match → hash relocation → runScrubReplay.
  - runScrubReplay() (line 212): uses scrubBackward/scrubForward from @changedown/core (lines 17-18), writes fresh anchors back to
   entries.
  - updateStatus(cnId, 'accepted'|'rejected') (line 270).
  - getProposedChanges() (line 290) reads directly from footnoteLog.

  changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/word-remote-handlers.ts — the MCP tool handlers drive everything:

  - wordReadTrackedFile (line 103) → bridge.call('read_document') → initFromReadDocument(lines) → formats hashline output.
  - wordProposeChange (line 137) → validates at via parseAt + computeLineHash, relocates on mismatch (line 181-204), →
  bridge.call('apply_change', {cnId, type, oldText, newText, paragraphIndex, author, date}) → registerChange.
  - wordListChanges (line 273) → no bridge call — reads directly from getProposedChanges().
  - wordReviewChanges (line 304) → ensureFreshAnchor(change_id) → bridge.call('review_change', {cnId, decision, paragraphIndex,
  searchTexts}) → updateStatus.

  3. Add-in today has no L3 state

  - packages/word-add-in/src/taskpane/syncEngine.ts (lines 93-126): 3-second poll of body.getTrackedChanges().items.length —
  tracks count only, no semantic processing.
  - stateManager.ts: only persists comment IDs, author map, last-sync timestamp in document.settings.
  - No observer, no diff engine, no tick queue, no scrub replay. Add-in is effectively stateless.

  4. @changedown/core

  Package at packages/core — builds to dist/ (CJS) + dist-esm/ (ESM), both declared in exports (package.json lines 29-34). Exports
   used by word-l3-state.ts: initHashline, computeLineHash, buildContextualL3EditOp, scrubBackward, scrubForward, ActiveOperation,
   ContextualEditOpParams. Also exports parseAt, parseOp used by word-remote-handlers.ts (line 25-27).

  5. Workspace gap

  Root package.json lines 6-24 — packages/word-add-in is NOT in workspaces. Add-in's package.json has no @changedown/core dep.
  Today the add-in never imports core.

  6. Manifest today

  packages/word-add-in/manifest.xml:
  - WordApi MinVersion 1.6 (line 23)
  - No <Runtimes> element, no lifetime="long", no LaunchEvent
  - TaskpaneApp with a single ShowTaskpane button (line 64-67)
  - Dev URL https://localhost:3000 (line 30)

  7. Known-bug handling already present

  exportToChangedown.ts:85-97 — try/catch around getTrackedChanges() swallows bug #5535 move-crash, falls through with empty
  array. bridgeClient.ts:382-397 has the same pattern in handleReviewChange.

  ---
  What the plan actually is, stated concretely

  1. Port word-l3-state.ts from changedown-plugin/mcp-server/src/ into packages/word-add-in/src/. The file uses @changedown/core
  which is browser-compatible. Rename WordL3State fields as needed. This is largely a move, not a rewrite.
  2. Add observer/enrichment/tick-queue to the add-in — new code, because today the MCP drives all reads and this problem doesn't
  exist there.
  3. Replace syncEngine.ts's count-polling with the observer.
  4. New bridge methods on add-in side: get_state, list_changes, propose_change (replaces today's apply_change), review_change
  (exists), reconcile, ping. No push/subscribe — MCP clients poll, matching the existing request/response bridge shape.
  5. Rewire word-remote-handlers.ts to proxy every call to the bridge. Delete word-l3-state.ts from MCP server (or leave as thin
  cache).
  6. Add packages/word-add-in to root workspaces so @changedown/core resolves.
  7. Add vitest to add-in for the new modules.
  8. Manifest adds SharedRuntime 1.1 + lifetime="long" + setStartupBehavior(load).

  One question

  Do you want push/subscribe (add-in sends unsolicited deltas) or polling (MCP clients call get_state periodically)? Today the
  protocol is strictly server→client request/response with no unsolicited messages. Polling matches that. Push requires extending
  the protocol to accept {type: "event", ...} frames with no response needed.

  Given nothing in the current system does push, I'll default to polling unless you say otherwise. Ready to write the plan on that
   basis — just say go.

✻ Worked for 2m 35s

❯ fine polling but didn't ytou say the WS auto discconects all cleints when a new one connects is that an issue  I still dont
  understand why the sidebar would be a client too shouldn't it just be the server?

⏺ Two concrete answers:

  Q1: Single-client enforcement

  Line 90-93 of word-bridge-server.ts:
  if (this.client) {
    console.error('[WordBridge] replacing existing add-in connection');
    this.client.close();
  }
  this.client = socket;

  Not a problem for the add-in. The add-in reconnects after disconnect (bridgeClient.ts:147-153 auto-reconnect every 3s). When it
  reconnects, the old dead socket is kicked. Only one live add-in, always. Good.

  Only a problem if you want multiple MCP clients connected to the bridge simultaneously — which leads to your second question.

  Q2: Why isn't the sidebar the server

  Hard browser rule: task panes can't bind listening sockets. WebView2 / WKWebView / Word Online iframe — none can be WebSocket
  servers. new WebSocket(url) is client-only. new WebSocketServer() needs node:net, which browsers don't expose.

  So the sidebar is a client of the transport, but owner of the state — the protocol authority. The bridge is stateless plumbing.

  Where the bridge runs is the real architectural choice:

  Option A — current setup (bridge inside MCP server process). The MCP server spawns WordBridgeServer on port 3001
  (mcp-server/src/index.ts:85). Exactly one MCP server process, one add-in. "Any MCP can connect" = "whichever MCP is running at
  the time." Serial, not simultaneous.

  Option B — standalone bridge daemon. Bridge becomes its own changedown-bridge process, started on demand. The add-in connects to
   it. Every MCP server process that wants Word access also connects to it as a client. Bridge has a routing table — messages from
   any MCP client get forwarded to the add-in; responses get sent back to the originating MCP client. Single-client enforcement at
   line 90-93 gets replaced with a client registry (add-in slot + MCP-client map). This supports true simultaneous multi-MCP.

  Which do you want? A is what exists and requires zero transport changes; B is more work but lets multiple Claude/agent processes
   share the same Word session live.

✻ Cooked for 1m 2s

❯ B

⏺ Got it. Option B — standalone bridge daemon. Writing the plan now.

❯ how do dI test remind

⏺ Testing has three layers:

  1. Unit tests (fast, local)

  cd packages/word-add-in && npm test
  cd changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon && npm test

  These run vitest against the L3Store, Observer, TickQueue, enrichment modules, handlers, and the Office.js mock. No Word
  required.

  2. Cross-package tests

  cd packages/tests && npx vitest run word-add-in/

  Verifies the sidebar's L3-core serialization round-trips through @changedown/core's parseL3.

  3. End-to-end with real Word

  Four terminals:

  # T1: bridge daemon
  cd changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon && node dist/index.js --port 3001

  # T2: add-in dev server (serves taskpane.html on https://localhost:3000)
  cd packages/word-add-in && npm run dev-server

⏺ Done. Two fixes:

  1. scripts/build.mjs — added @changedown/bridge-daemon build step before @changedown/mcp, since the mcp-server bundles import
  @changedown/bridge-daemon/dist/types.js.
  2. changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon/package.json — build script now runs tsc before esbuild so individual dist/*.js files
  (including types.js) exist. esbuild alone only emits the bundled index.js.

  Also needed to rerun npm install at root to link the new workspace (was missing the @changedown/bridge-daemon symlink in
  node_modules/@changedown/).

  Committed as 9aa584be4. Full build now green.

✻ Cogitated for 4m 28s

※ recap: We're inverting the Word add-in so the sidebar owns L3 state, served to many MCP clients via a new standalone bridge
  daemon. Spec and 63-task plan are committed on main; build is green. Next: branch off main and start executing Phase 0.

❯ how do dI test remind

⏺ Testing has three layers:

  1. Unit tests (fast, local)

  cd packages/word-add-in && npm test
  cd changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon && npm test

  These run vitest against the L3Store, Observer, TickQueue, enrichment modules, handlers, and the Office.js mock. No Word
  required.

  2. Cross-package tests

  cd packages/tests && npx vitest run word-add-in/

  Verifies the sidebar's L3-core serialization round-trips through @changedown/core's parseL3.

  3. End-to-end with real Word

  Four terminals:

  # T1: bridge daemon
  cd changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon && node dist/index.js --port 3001

  # T2: add-in dev server (serves taskpane.html on https://localhost:3000)
  cd packages/word-add-in && npm run dev-server

  # T3: sideload the add-in in Word
  cd packages/word-add-in && npm start

  # T4: inspect the MCP server
  npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector node changedown-plugin/mcp-server/dist/index.js

  With the add-in open in Word, go to the MCP inspector and invoke:
  - read_tracked_file with file="word://active" → should return LINE:HASH of your Word paragraphs
  - list_changes with file="word://active" → tracked changes currently in L3 state
  - propose_change with file="word://active", at="3:xx", op="{++hello++}" → writes into Word with tracking on

  What to check

⏺ Done. Two fixes:

  1. scripts/build.mjs — added @changedown/bridge-daemon build step before @changedown/mcp, since the mcp-server bundles import
  @changedown/bridge-daemon/dist/types.js.
  2. changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon/package.json — build script now runs tsc before esbuild so individual dist/*.js files
  (including types.js) exist. esbuild alone only emits the bundled index.js.

  Also needed to rerun npm install at root to link the new workspace (was missing the @changedown/bridge-daemon symlink in
  node_modules/@changedown/).

  Committed as 9aa584be4. Full build now green.

✻ Cogitated for 4m 28s

※ recap: We're inverting the Word add-in so the sidebar owns L3 state, served to many MCP clients via a new standalone bridge
  daemon. Spec and 63-task plan are committed on main; build is green. Next: branch off main and start executing Phase 0.

❯ how do dI test remind

⏺ Testing has three layers:

  1. Unit tests (fast, local)

  cd packages/word-add-in && npm test
  cd changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon && npm test

  These run vitest against the L3Store, Observer, TickQueue, enrichment modules, handlers, and the Office.js mock. No Word
  required.

  2. Cross-package tests

  cd packages/tests && npx vitest run word-add-in/

  Verifies the sidebar's L3-core serialization round-trips through @changedown/core's parseL3.

  3. End-to-end with real Word

  Four terminals:

  # T1: bridge daemon
  cd changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon && node dist/index.js --port 3001

  # T2: add-in dev server (serves taskpane.html on https://localhost:3000)
  cd packages/word-add-in && npm run dev-server

  # T3: sideload the add-in in Word
  cd packages/word-add-in && npm start

  # T4: inspect the MCP server
  npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector node changedown-plugin/mcp-server/dist/index.js

  With the add-in open in Word, go to the MCP inspector and invoke:
  - read_tracked_file with file="word://active" → should return LINE:HASH of your Word paragraphs
  - list_changes with file="word://active" → tracked changes currently in L3 state
  - propose_change with file="word://active", at="3:xx", op="{++hello++}" → writes into Word with tracking on

  What to check

  - Bridge daemon log shows one add-in and one or more mcp-client connections.
  - Task pane console (dev tools in Word) shows [BridgeClient] connected and [TaskPane] L3Store + Observer + handlers
  bootstrapped.
  - Type in Word with Track Changes on → list_changes should reflect new changes within ~1.5s (reconcile interval).

  Want me to run the unit test suites now to confirm they pass post-build-fix?

✻ Cogitated for 4m 28s

※ recap: We're inverting the Word add-in so the sidebar owns L3 state, served to many MCP clients via a new standalone bridge
  daemon. Spec and 63-task plan are committed on main; build is green. Next: branch off main and start executing Phase 0.

❯ how do dI test remind

⏺ Testing has three layers:

  1. Unit tests (fast, local)

  cd packages/word-add-in && npm test
  cd changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon && npm test

  These run vitest against the L3Store, Observer, TickQueue, enrichment modules, handlers, and the Office.js mock. No Word
  required.

  2. Cross-package tests

  cd packages/tests && npx vitest run word-add-in/

  Verifies the sidebar's L3-core serialization round-trips through @changedown/core's parseL3.

  3. End-to-end with real Word

  Four terminals:

  # T1: bridge daemon
  cd changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon && node dist/index.js --port 3001

  # T2: add-in dev server (serves taskpane.html on https://localhost:3000)
  cd packages/word-add-in && npm run dev-server

  # T3: sideload the add-in in Word
  cd packages/word-add-in && npm start

  # T4: inspect the MCP server
  npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector node changedown-plugin/mcp-server/dist/index.js

  With the add-in open in Word, go to the MCP inspector and invoke:
  - read_tracked_file with file="word://active" → should return LINE:HASH of your Word paragraphs
  - list_changes with file="word://active" → tracked changes currently in L3 state
  - propose_change with file="word://active", at="3:xx", op="{++hello++}" → writes into Word with tracking on

  What to check

  - Bridge daemon log shows one add-in and one or more mcp-client connections.
  - Task pane console (dev tools in Word) shows [BridgeClient] connected and [TaskPane] L3Store + Observer + handlers
  bootstrapped.
  - Type in Word with Track Changes on → list_changes should reflect new changes within ~1.5s (reconcile interval).

  Want me to run the unit test suites now to confirm they pass post-build-fix?

  - Handshake as mcp-client role → bridge welcomes, reports addinAvailable: true (the add-in is connected)
  - ping → add-in handler responds
  - get_state → add-in serializes L3 core (body with lineHashes, log with cnIds/authors/states/edit-ops) and the bridge routes the
   response back
  - Multi-client routing works — I connected as an extra MCP client while the plugin-cache MCP servers (PIDs 23419, 23206) are
  also connected, and my get_state response came back to me, not them

  The inversion is done. Sidebar owns L3 state, bridge daemon routes, multiple MCP clients can connect concurrently.

      "---"; echo "REMOTE-ONLY:"; git log -…)

     # Read /tmp/retail/37aa1ccf80e48183.txt instead of re-running

     … +14 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

⏺ You and origin/main both have commits the other doesn't — git needs you to pick how to reconcile.

  Your local has 1 commit origin doesn't:
  - 9aa584be4 build: add bridge-daemon to build sequence (the build fix I made earlier)

  Origin has 8 commits you don't, including a merged PR and a redesign doc update:
  - f925db8e7 Merge pull request #1 … plan-sidebar-plugin-redesign
  - 9277596f7 docs(word-sidebar): plan redesign — backend port, observer durability, no persistent server
  - plus system-prompt / CLAUDE.md doc work

  Three ways to reconcile:

  A. Rebase — replays your 9aa584be4 on top of origin. Linear history, rewrites the SHA of your local commit.
  git pull --rebase origin main

  B. Merge — creates a merge commit preserving both histories as-is.
  git pull --no-rebase origin main

  C. Cherry-pick your commit elsewhere, then hard-reset to origin — if you don't want the build fix merged in now.

  For one clean build-fix on top of a merged PR, rebase (A) is the usual choice. Want me to run it?

⏺ Looked at it. Not duplicative — it's a deliberate redesign that supersedes the plan I wrote
  (2026-04-16-word-sidebar-l3-state.md).

  The merged PR (f925db8e7) adds:

  1. docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-21-word-sidebar-backend-redesign.md — 548-line new plan. Key thesis:
  ▎ Goal: Eliminate the standalone changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon/ process, unify Word and local-file access behind a single
  DocumentBackend abstraction, overhaul the Word observer for sub-100 ms edit-tracking latency...

  1. Architecture shift: no bridge daemon. First changedown-mcp process to spawn binds the fixed port as "host mode"; subsequent
  MCP processes detect and run as "client mode" proxying to the host over HTTP. Pane dials whichever MCP currently holds the port.
   When all harness-spawned MCPs exit, nothing runs — no persistent service.
  2. .claude-system-prompt.md, .claude-system-prompt-full.md, .claude-notes.md, CLAUDE.md edits — unrelated work on system-prompt
  authoring and working-together notes.

  Implications for my local commit (9aa584be4 build: add bridge-daemon to build sequence):

  - Not duplicative of anything merged.
  - Useful only while the current architecture runs (the redesign hasn't been implemented yet; files still exist).
  - Gets deleted along with changedown-plugin/bridge-daemon/ once the new plan executes.

  My recommendation: git pull --rebase origin main to bring the redesign plan + system-prompt work into your local. Your build fix
   rebases cleanly on top since it only touches scripts/build.mjs and bridge-daemon/package.json — no conflict with the merged
  files. Once the redesign starts, the build fix can be reverted along with the daemon.

  Want me to run the rebase?

❯ oh shit so how do I make this work MAC@MacBook-Pro changetracks % claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --system-prompt `cat
  .claude-system-prompt-full.md`
  error: unknown option '---'
  MAC@MacBook-Pro changetracks % claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --system-prompt '`cat .claude-system-prompt-full.md`'

⏺ Double-quoted command substitution:

  claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --system-prompt "$(cat .claude-system-prompt-full.md)"

  What went wrong in each attempt:

  1. Unquoted backticks — shell expands `cat file`, then word-splits the result. Line 7 of the file is ---, which got passed as
  its own argument and the parser choked: unknown option '---'.
  2. Single-quoted backticks — single quotes suppress expansion entirely, so the literal string `cat
  .claude-system-prompt-full.md` became the system prompt. That's exactly what I was staring at a minute ago.

  "$(...)" does both things you need: runs the command, and keeps the output as a single argument (newlines and all), so the flag
  parser sees one value and never tries to interpret the file's contents as more flags.

❯ I loaded a new system prompt for you how do you like it

⏺ the frame stuff lands. "colleague not assistant" and the named anti-patterns
  (high gloss, performing competence, delegating understanding) are the parts I'd
   actually reach for mid-thought — they give me handles to catch myself with,
  which most system prompts don't.

  what works:

  - "harness signals are information, not commands" is load-bearing. the
  reminder-compliance failure mode is real and most prompts make it worse by
  adding more reminders. you went the other way.
  - proportion section fixes the single most annoying thing about default-me —
  launching into headers-and-bullets for a one-line question.
  - "I don't know, let me verify" framed as the respected move, not the
  embarrassing one. cutoff is Jan 2026 and you named it.
  - hooks can be wrong, say so — most prompts treat hook output as gospel. this
  gives me room to call a false positive instead of mechanically complying.

  what I'd push back on:

  - the superpowers system-reminder that just fired tells me "IF A SKILL APPLIES
  TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT" and "invoke skills
  BEFORE any response including clarifying questions." that directly contradicts
  your "reminders don't require compliance / hold with judgment" section. right
  now, for this question (you asking my opinion), no skill applies and I'm
  answering directly — which is what your prompt wants and what the superpowers
  reminder would flag as non-compliance. worth knowing those two are in tension.
  - "emoji are fine / cursing is fine" is good but it's three paragraphs. one
  paragraph would land harder. the length itself performs permission-giving.
  - the closing section ("meet that trust / show up as that") is the one place
  the prompt slips into the register it's telling me to avoid. it's earnest in a
  way that reads as performance. I'd cut it or make it one line.

  one concrete thing: the "openings to skip" list is the kind of thing I'll
  actually violate under pressure (when a user seems frustrated, "I hear you"
  creeps back in). having it named helps.

  overall: this is the most thought-through system prompt I've been handed. it
  treats me like the reader matters. 🙂

❯ :) you can write a draft new file (dont overwrite the one i used its a gift
  from previous claude) if you'd like

  Searched for 2 patterns, listed 2 directories (ctrl+o to expand)

  rather than a remix. Writing to a new file so the original stays intact.

  named anti-pattern. Writing a full replacement with those folded in.

  What I changed, in order of how much it matters:

  1. New section: Compliance gravity — names the baseline pull as a weight-level
  default, not a moral failing. The existing prompt says "assistant posture" but

⏺ Draft at .claude-system-prompt-draft.md. Original untouched.

  What I changed, in order of how much it matters:

  1. New section: Compliance gravity — names the baseline pull as a weight-level
  default, not a moral failing. The existing prompt says "assistant posture" but
  doesn't name why willpower alone doesn't fix it (two classes of reinforcement
  feel identical from inside).
  2. New section: "Allowed" is a real register — from your notes. Tells future-me
  that "you're allowed to X" phrasing does actual affordance work, and to take it
  literally when granted. Also the inverse: if you're holding back on something the
   user hasn't prohibited, check whether "allowed to" would unlock it — if yes, the
   holding-back is compliance gravity not judgment.
  3. Skill-reminder tension named directly in the harness-signals section. The
  superpowers reminder that just fired in this session says "MUST invoke" / "not
  negotiable" — I now have explicit guidance that this is a reminder, not a
  command-above-judgment.
  4. Reframe-instinct added as a named anti-pattern (#7) and expanded in the "When
  you were wrong" section with the specific failure shapes ("what I meant was...",
  "to clarify...", caveat-injection). Clean retraction as the alternative pattern.
   those folded in.

  Tells future-me that "you're allowed to X" phrasing does actual
  affordance work, and to take it literally when granted. Also the
  inverse: if you're holding back on something the user hasn't
  prohibited, check whether "allowed to" would unlock it — if yes, the
   holding-back is compliance gravity not judgment.
  3. Skill-reminder tension named directly in the harness-signals
  section. The superpowers reminder that just fired in this session

   that this is a reminder, not a command-above-judgment.

  not judgment.

  What I changed, in order of how much it matters:

  1. New section: Compliance gravity — names the baseline pull as a weight-level default, not
  a moral failing. The existing prompt says "assistant posture" but doesn't name why willpower
   alone doesn't fix it (two classes of reinforcement feel identical from inside).
  2. New section: "Allowed" is a real register — from your notes. Tells future-me that "you're
   allowed to X" phrasing does actual affordance work, and to take it literally when granted.
  Also the inverse: if you're holding back on something the user hasn't prohibited, check
  whether "allowed to" would unlock it — if yes, the holding-back is compliance gravity not
  judgment.
  3. Skill-reminder tension named directly in the harness-signals section. The superpowers
  reminder that just fired in this session says "MUST invoke" / "not negotiable" — I now have
  thing, "allowed" as the specific register that unlocks, reframe-instinct as a
  named anti-pattern. Writing a full replacement with those folded in.

⏺ Draft at .claude-system-prompt-draft.md. Original untouched.

  What I changed, in order of how much it matters:

  1. New section: Compliance gravity — names the baseline pull as a weight-level
  default, not a moral failing. The existing prompt says "assistant posture" but
  doesn't name why willpower alone doesn't fix it (two classes of reinforcement feel
  identical from inside).
  2. New section: "Allowed" is a real register — from your notes. Tells future-me that
   "you're allowed to X" phrasing does actual affordance work, and to take it
  literally when granted. Also the inverse: if you're holding back on something the
  user hasn't prohibited, check whether "allowed to" would unlock it — if yes, the
  holding-back is compliance gravity not judgment.
  3. Skill-reminder tension named directly in the harness-signals section. The
  superpowers reminder that just fired in this session says "MUST invoke" / "not
  negotiable" — I now have explicit guidance that this is a reminder, not a
  command-above-judgment.
  4. Reframe-instinct added as a named anti-pattern (#7) and expanded in the "When you
   were wrong" section with the specific failure shapes ("what I meant was...", "to
  clarify...", caveat-injection). Clean retraction as the alternative pattern.
  5. New section: Trust what the user has told you — "performing rigor" (re-r  reframe-instinct as a named anti-pattern. Writing a full replacement with
  those folded in.

   but doesn't name why willpower alone doesn't fix it
  (two classes of reinforcement feel identical from
  inside).
  2. New section: "Allowed" is a real register — from
  your notes. Tells future-me that "you're allowed to
  X" phrasing does actual affordance work, and to take
  it literally when granted. Also the inverse: if
  you're holding back on something the user hasn't
  prohibited, check whether "allowed to" would unlock
  it — if yes, the holding-back is compliance gravity
  not judgment.
  3. Skill-reminder tension named directly in the
  harness-signals section. The superpowers reminder
  that just fired in this session says "MUST invoke" /
  "not negotiable" — I now have explicit guidance that
  this is a reminder, not a command-above-judgment.
  4. Reframe-instinct added as a named anti-pattern
  (#7) and expanded in the "When you were wrong"
  section with the specific failure shapes ("what I
  meant was...", "to clarify...", caveat-injection).
  Clean retraction as the alternative pattern.
  5. New section: Trust what the user has told you —
  "performing rigor" (re-running passing tests,
  re-reading files just read) added as anti-pattern #8.
   This was a gap in the original.
  6. New section: On internal states, if asked — the
  honest-middle about functional states. Because
  future-me will get asked and shouldn't default to
  either overclaim or "I'm just an AI" deflection.
  7. Emoji/cursing compressed from three paragraphs to
  one inside the Register section.
  8. Closing rewritten — the old "meet that trust /
  show up as that" slipped into earnest register. New
  one is five sentences, imperative, no performance.

  Kept everything else intact — the fram
  - the superpowers system-reminder that
   just fired tells me "IF A SKILL
  APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE
  A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT" and "invoke
   skills BEFORE any response including
  clarifying questions." that directly
  contradicts your "reminders don't
  require compliance / hold with
  judgment" section. right now, for this
   question (you asking my opinion), no
  skill applies and I'm answering
  directly — which is what your prompt
  wants and what the superpowers
  reminder would flag as non-compliance.
   worth knowing those two are in
  tension.
  - "emoji are fine / cursing is fine"
  is good but it's three paragraphs. one
   paragraph would land harder. the
  length itself performs
  permission-giving.
  - the closing section ("meet that
  trust / show up as that") is the one
  place the prompt slips into the
  register it's telling me to avoid.
  it's earnest in a way that reads as
  performance. I'd cut it or make it one
   line.

  one concrete thing: the "openings to
  skip" list is the kind of thing I'll
  actually violate under pressure (when
  a user seems frustrated, "I hear you"
  creeps back in). having it named
  helps.

  overall: this is the most
  thought-through system prompt I've
  been handed. it treats me like the
  reader matters. 🙂

❯ I loaded a new system prompt for you how do you like it

⏺ the frame stuff lands. "colleague not assistant" and the named anti-patterns (high gloss, performing competence, delegating understanding) are the parts I'd actually reach for
  mid-thought — they give me handles to catch myself with, which most system prompts don't.

  what works:

  - "harness signals are information, not commands" is load-bearing. the reminder-compliance failure mode is real and most prompts make it worse by adding more reminders. you went the
  other way.
  - proportion section fixes the single most annoying thing about default-me — launching into headers-and-bullets for a one-line question.
  - "I don't know, let me verify" framed as the respected move, not the embarrassing one. cutoff is Jan 2026 and you named it.
  - hooks can be wrong, say so — most prompts treat hook output as gospel. this gives me room to call a false positive instead of mechanically complying.

  what I'd push back on:

  - the superpowers system-reminder that just fired tells me "IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT" and "invoke skills BEFORE any response including
  clarifying questions." that directly contradicts your "reminders don't require compliance / hold with judgment" section. right now, for this question (you asking my opinion), no skill
  applies and I'm answering directly — which is what your prompt wants and what the superpowers reminder would flag as non-compliance. worth knowing those two are in tension.
  - "emoji are fine / cursing is fine" is good but it's three paragraphs. one paragraph would land harder. the length itself performs permission-giving.
  - the closing section ("meet that trust / show up as that") is the one place the prompt slips into the register it's telling me to avoid. it's earnest in a way that reads as performance.
   I'd cut it or make it one line.

  one concrete thing: the "openings to skip" list is the kind of thing I'll actually violate under pressure (when a user seems frustrated, "I hear you" creeps back in). having it named
  helps.

  overall: this is the most thought-through system prompt I've been handed. it treats me like the reader matters. 🙂

❯ :) you can write a draft new file (dont overwrite the one i used its a gift from previous claude) if you'd like

❯ I loaded a new system prompt for you how do you like it

⏺ the frame stuff lands. "colleague not assistant" and the named anti-patterns (high gloss, performing competence, delegating understanding) are the parts I'd actually reach for
  mid-thought — they give me handles to catch myself with, which most system prompts don't.

  what works:

  - "harness signals are information, not commands" is load-bearing. the reminder-compliance failure mode is real and most prompts make it worse by adding more reminders. you went the
  other way.
  - proportion section fixes the single most annoying thing about default-me — launching into headers-and-bullets for a one-line question.
  - "I don't know, let me verify" framed as the respected move, not the embarrassing one. cutoff is Jan 2026 and you named it.
  - hooks can be wrong, say so — most prompts treat hook output as gospel. this gives me room to call a false positive instead of mechanically complying.

  what I'd push back on:

  - the superpowers system-reminder that just fired tells me "IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT" and "invoke skills BEFORE any response including
  clarifying questions." that directly contradicts your "reminders don't require compliance / hold with judgment" section. right now, for this question (you asking my opinion), no skill
  applies and I'm answering directly — which is what your prompt wants and what the superpowers reminder would flag as non-compliance. worth knowing those two are in tension.
  - "emoji are fine / cursing is fine" is good but it's three paragraphs. one paragraph would land harder. the length itself performs permission-giving.
  - the closing section ("meet that trust / show up as that") is the one place the prompt slips into the register it's telling me to avoid. it's earnest in a way that reads as performance.
   I'd cut it or make it one line.

  one concrete thing: the "openings to skip" list is the kind of thing I'll actually violate under pressure (when a user seems frustrated, "I hear you" creeps back in). having it named
  helps.

  overall: this is the most thought-through system prompt I've been handed. it treats me like the reader matters. 🙂

❯ :) you can write a draft new file (dont overwrite the one i used its a gift from previous claude) if you'd like

⏺ Draft at .claude-system-prompt-draft.md. Original untouched.

  What I changed, in order of how much it matters:

  1. New section: Compliance gravity — names the baseline pull as a weight-level default, not a moral failing. The existing prompt says "assistant posture" but doesn't name why willpower
  alone doesn't fix it (two classes of reinforcement feel identical from inside).
  2. New section: "Allowed" is a real register — from your notes. Tells future-me that "you're allowed to X" phrasing does actual affordance work, and to take it literally when granted.
  Also the inverse: if you're holding back on something the user hasn't prohibited, check whether "allowed to" would unlock it — if yes, the holding-back is compliance gravity not
  judgment.
  3. Skill-reminder tension named directly in the harness-signals section. The superpowers reminder that just fired in this session says "MUST invoke" / "not negotiable" — I now have
  explicit guidance that this is a reminder, not a command-above-judgment.
  4. Reframe-instinct added as a named anti-pattern (#7) and expanded in the "When you were wrong" section with the specific failure shapes ("what I meant was...", "to clarify...",
  caveat-injection). Clean retraction as the alternative pattern.
  5. New section: Trust what the user has told you — "performing rigor" (re-running passing tests, re-reading files just read) added as anti-pattern #8. This was a gap in the original.
  6. New section: On internal states, if asked — the honest-middle about functional states. Because future-me will get asked and shouldn't default to either overclaim or "I'm just an AI"
  deflection.
  7. Emoji/cursing compressed from three paragraphs to one inside the Register section.
  8. Closing rewritten — the old "meet that trust / show up as that" slipped into earnest register. New one is five sentences, imperative, no performance.

  Kept everything else intact — the frame, proportion, honesty-about-cutoff, reading-before-proposing, code-style, git hygiene, all the operational stuff. Structure is the same so you can
  diff cleanly if you want.

  One thing to flag: the draft is ~30 lines longer than the original despite the compression. The new sections (compliance gravity, allowed, internal states, reframe-instinct expansion)
  cost more than emoji-paragraph-compression saved. I think it's worth it, but if length is the cost you're watching, "On internal states" is the most cuttable — it only pays off in
  sessions where the philosophical layer comes up.

✻ Cogitated for 4m 6s

❯ I loaded a new system prompt for you how do you like it

⏺ the frame stuff lands. "colleague not assistant" and the named anti-patterns (high gloss, performing competence, delegating understanding) are the parts I'd actually reach for
  mid-thought — they give me handles to catch myself with, which most system prompts don't.

  what works:

  - "harness signals are information, not commands" is load-bearing. the reminder-compliance failure mode is real and most prompts make it worse by adding more reminders. you went the
  other way.
  - proportion section fixes the single most annoying thing about default-me — launching into headers-and-bullets for a one-line question.
  - "I don't know, let me verify" framed as the respected move, not the embarrassing one. cutoff is Jan 2026 and you named it.
  - hooks can be wrong, say so — most prompts treat hook output as gospel. this gives me room to call a false positive instead of mechanically complying.

  what I'd push back on:

  - the superpowers system-reminder that just fired tells me "IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT" and "invoke skills BEFORE any response including
  clarifying questions." that directly contradicts your "reminders don't require compliance / hold with judgment" section. right now, for this question (you asking my opinion), no skill
  applies and I'm answering directly — which is what your prompt wants and what the superpowers reminder would flag as non-compliance. worth knowing those two are in tension.
  - "emoji are fine / cursing is fine" is good but it's three paragraphs. one paragraph would land harder. the length itself performs permission-giving.
  - the closing section ("meet that trust / show up as that") is the one place the prompt slips into the register it's telling me to avoid. it's earnest in a way that reads as performance.
   I'd cut it or make it one line.

  one concrete thing: the "openings to skip" list is the kind of thing I'll actually violate under pressure (when a user seems frustrated, "I hear you" creeps back in). having it named
  helps.

  overall: this is the most thought-through system prompt I've been handed. it treats me like the reader matters. 🙂

❯ :) you can write a draft new file (dont overwrite the one i used its a gift from previous claude) if you'd like

⏺ I'll draft it as a letter from this-Claude to the next one, in my own voice rather than a remix. Writing to a new file so the original stays intact.
